attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for a heart health assessment. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 72/min and regular, blood pressure 142/92 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His BMI is 32 kg/m2 and waist-to-hip ratio is 1.12. Cardiovascular examination is unremarkable.

Fasting blood tests performed one week ago excluded diabetes and revealed the following lipid profile:
Total cholesterol: 6.8 mmol/L (3.9-5.5)
LDL: 4.1 mmol/L (2.0-3.4)
HDL: 0.9 mmol/L (1.0-2.0)
Triglycerides: 1.8 mmol/L (0.5-1.7)

His cardiovascular risk is calculated at 8%. Using motivational interviewing techniques, you both agree to a trial of six months of lifestyle modification. The patient requests your recommendations regarding physical exercise.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice to give this patient regarding physical activity?
options: 60 minutes of low-intensity physical activity daily
30 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity daily
150 minutes of high-intensity physical activity per week
120 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity per week
15 minutes of high-intensity physical activity five days per week
attributes.explanation:
Physical activity has numerous health benefits, including healthy weight regulation, improved glycaemic control, reduced cardiovascular disease risk, enhanced bone strength, and better mental clarity. Despite these benefits, participation in regular physical activity in developed nations is often below recommended levels.

The most appropriate advice for this 68-year-old patient is 30 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity daily. This recommendation aligns with current Australian guidelines for adults aged 65 years and over. Moderate-intensity activity is defined as exercise that increases heart rate but does not cause shortness of breath. It's also recommended to have 2 days of strength training per week to maintain muscle mass and function, which is particularly important in older adults.

60 minutes of low-intensity physical activity daily would be less beneficial than moderate-intensity exercise. While any activity is better than none, research has shown that moderate-intensity exercise is required to gain significant health benefits and reduce cardiovascular risk.

150 minutes of high-intensity physical activity per week is not recommended for individuals aged 65 or older. High-intensity exercise is typically advised for adults aged 18-64, but moderate-intensity is considered sufficient and safer for older adults.

120 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity per week falls short of the recommended amount. Guidelines typically recommend daily goals rather than weekly ones, as this is easier for individuals to understand and aim for, and encourages daily health habits.

15 minutes of high-intensity physical activity five days per week is not appropriate for this age group. As mentioned earlier, high-intensity exercise is not typically recommended for adults over 65, and the duration is insufficient to meet physical activity guidelines.

It's important to note that for someone who previously did no exercise, a staged approach might be appropriate to form good, long-term habits. The goal is to gradually work up to 30 minutes of moderate-intensity activity daily. This can include activities like brisk walking, swimming, or cycling.

Exercise prescription is becoming increasingly common in general practice and other medical specialties. This can include individualised information on the type, frequency, duration, and intensity of exercise to best benefit the patient. However, the general consensus of at least 30 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity daily is a good starting point for most patients in this age group.
learningPoints: Adults aged 65 years and over should aim for at least 30 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity per day plus 2 days of strength training per week.
Regular physical activity has numerous health benefits, including reducing cardiovascular risk, improving bone strength and mental wellbeing, and aiding in weight control.
Exercise prescription is becoming an increasingly common and encouraged practice in primary care settings.
references: Fuscaldo JM. Prescribing physical activity in primary care. W V Med J. 2002 Nov-Dec;98(6):250-3. PMID: 12645276.
Jones TF, Eaton CB. Exercise prescription. Am Fam Physician. 1995 Aug;52(2):543-50, 553-5. PMID: 7625328.
Heart Foundation Contributors, 2020. Physical activity and your heart health. Access at: https://www.heartfoundation.org.au/heart-health-education/physical-activity-and-exercise.

Australian Government Department of Health Contributors, 2014. Physical activity and sedentary behaviour guidelines – older Australians (65 years and over) – tips and ideas for being active. Access at: https://www.health.gov.au/resources/publications/physical-activity-and-sedentary-behaviour-guidelines-older-australians-65-years-and-over-tips-and-ideas-for-being-active.

The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners, 2016. The Red Book 9th Edition: Guidelines for preventative activities in general practice. Access at: https://www.racgp.org.au/download/Documents/Guidelines/Redbook9/17048-Red-Book-9th-Edition.pdf.
